region_of_origin: Southeast Asia (Indonesia, Malaysia, Philippines); widely cultivated in Thailand, Malaysia, Indonesia; sold year-round in Singapore
food_subgroup: Fresh tropical fruit — purple pericarp (rind) removed, white segmented flesh eaten; 1 fruit ≈ 40–50 g flesh; 2-fruit serving ≈ 80–100 g flesh

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for mangosteen flesh only. FOODON_03301710 covers mangosteen as a whole fruit; closest parent used. The specific flesh-only preparation is not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mangosteen — FoodEx2 Level 3 fruit code for Garcinia mangostana
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers mangosteen fruit broadly; flesh-only preparation not separately distinguished at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for mangosteen flesh. Not coded to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of mangosteen is 2 whole fruits, yielding approximately 80–100 g of edible white flesh after removal of the thick purple pericarp.

nutrient_source.title: Mangosteen — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and FAO/INFOODS SEA
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition table for mangosteen (Garcinia mangostana), fresh flesh. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Tropical Fruits — University of Sydney GI Database reference values; FAO/INFOODS SEA contextual confirmation
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~40 for mangosteen is referenced from University of Sydney GI Database tropical fruit values. Mangosteen is not always individually listed; the value of ~40 is consistent with published peer-reviewed references for Garcinia mangostana and is corroborated by FAO/INFOODS SEA contextual data. Confidence 0.9. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 16.0) / 100 = 6.4 for a 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (7 mg/100 g); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Mangosteen flesh is very low in sodium (7 mg/100 g) and contains modest potassium (48 mg/100 g), making it a safe fruit choice for individuals managing hypertension; no sodium-related restriction is required.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and low glycemic load per serving (~6.4)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 40 and a GL of ~6.4 per 100 g serving, mangosteen is among the better glycemic choices in the tropical fruit category for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the small individual fruit size and relatively higher cost per fruit naturally moderate portion intake, and it compares favourably to higher-GI tropical fruits such as durian, lychee, and ripe banana.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, low sodium, and presence of dietary fibre and folate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mangosteen flesh is very low in saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), contains no cholesterol, and provides dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g) and folate (31 mcg/100 g), all of which are consistent with a heart-healthy dietary pattern; regular consumption as part of a varied fruit intake poses no cardiovascular concern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Xanthone content (alpha-mangostin, gamma-mangostin) with demonstrated in-vitro anti-inflammatory activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mangosteen flesh contains xanthones (alpha-mangostin, gamma-mangostin) that have demonstrated inhibition of COX-2, TNF-alpha, IL-6, and IL-1beta in in-vitro studies relevant to RA inflammation; however, the highest xanthone concentration is in the inedible purple pericarp (rind), and the flesh provides a lower but still meaningful xanthone exposure — regular consumption is considered beneficial rather than harmful for RA patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mangosteen (山竹; Garcinia mangostana) is Southeast Asia's celebrated 'Queen of Fruits' — a tropical fruit native to Indonesia, Malaysia, and the Philippines, widely cultivated across Thailand and Malaysia and sold year-round at Singapore wet markets and supermarkets. The fruit has a thick, deep-purple inedible pericarp (rind) enclosing brilliant white, segmented flesh of a uniquely delicate sweet-acid flavour that is widely regarded as one of the finest of all tropical fruits. Only the white flesh is eaten; the rind is discarded. A standard serving is 2 whole fruits, yielding approximately 80–100 g of edible flesh. Nutritionally, mangosteen flesh is a moderate-carbohydrate, low-fat fruit: 63 kcal/100 g, 16 g carbohydrate, 14 g sugar, 1.8 g dietary fibre, 0.6 g protein, and 0.6 g fat per 100 g. Its glycemic index is approximately 40 (low GI), giving a glycemic load of ~6.4 per 100 g serving — making it one of the better glycemic choices among tropical fruits for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium is very low at 7 mg/100 g, making it suitable for hypertension management. Mangosteen is particularly notable for its xanthone content — alpha-mangostin and gamma-mangostin — polyphenolic compounds concentrated in the purple pericarp that have shown significant anti-inflammatory activity in cell and animal studies, including inhibition of COX-2, TNF-alpha, IL-6, and IL-1beta, all central mediators of rheumatoid arthritis inflammation. The flesh itself contains lower but still meaningful xanthone levels. Mangosteen is vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database and FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition table — both approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas source policy. GI value of ~40 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database tropical fruit references. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for mangosteen flesh not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA table as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the primary sources consulted. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB/FAO source). cholesterol_mg reported as 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based fruit). vitamin_a_mcg_rae reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible per HPB/FAO source). vitamin_b12_mcg reported as 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food). vitamin_d_mcg reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible per HPB/FAO source). FoodEx2 code A00LC used as closest parent for mangosteen fruit; flesh-only preparation not separately coded at this level. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 used as closest parent; no flesh-specific concept exists. SNOMED CT not coded to avoid fabrication. GI ~40 is an analogous estimate consistent with University of Sydney GI Database tropical fruit values; confidence 0.9. Xanthone anti-inflammatory properties are primarily associated with the pericarp (rind), not the flesh eaten in standard consumption.
